Updated task #3 status

160	
161	/// Install a per-proc TTBR0 + ASID and invalidate stale TLB entries for
162	/// that ASID. Called from `proc::sched::schedule_next` on every EL1 → EL0
163	/// transition that picks a proc with `ttbr0_pa != 0`.
164	///
165	/// `ttbr0_pa` is the L0-root PA, frame-aligned. `asid` is in [1, 255];
166	/// caller is responsible for sourcing it from
167	/// [`super::asid::alloc_asid`].
168	///
169	/// The TLBI is unconditional. With three ASIDs in slice 3.1b, the cost is
170	/// negligible (~tens of cycles per switch on QEMU) and the simpler
171	/// control flow is worth more than micro-optimization. A later slice can
172	/// elide it once an "AS already activated" flag is added per AddrSpace.
173	///
174	/// Writes only TTBR0_EL1 — TTBR1 stays owned by Limine + the kernel's
175	/// translation regime. The kernel never installs a custom TTBR1.
176	///
177	/// SAFETY: must be called with DAIF masked at EL1, single-threaded boot.
178	/// `ttbr0_pa` must point at a valid L0 page table the caller owns.
179	pub unsafe fn switch_ttbr0_with_asid(ttbr0_pa: u64, asid: u8) {
180	    // Hard guards (not debug_assert!): the kernel only ever builds --release,
181	    // where debug_assert! is compiled out. A null/zero ASID here means a proc
182	    // with no address space reached the scheduler; installing TTBR0_EL1 = 0
183	    // would point the EL0 walk at PA 0. Fail loudly at the choke point.
184	    assert!(asid != 0, "switch_ttbr0_with_asid: ASID 0 is reserved");
185	    assert!(
186	        ttbr0_pa != 0 && ttbr0_pa & (PAGE_SIZE as u64 - 1) == 0,
187	        "switch_ttbr0_with_asid: ttbr0_pa {ttbr0_pa:#x} null or not page-aligned",
188	    );
189	    let tagged = ttbr0_pa | ((asid as u64) << 48);
190	    // `tlbi aside1, Xt` consumes the ASID from bits [63:48] of Xt; the
191	    // remaining bits are RES0 / SBZ. We pass the same `asid << 48` we
192	    // used for TTBR0 — keeps the encoding self-documenting.
193	    //
194	    // SAFETY: TTBR0_EL1 and TLBI ASIDE1 are EL1 ops with no normal-memory
195	    // access. The ISB after MSR ensures the new TTBR0 is observed before
196	    // the TLBI; the DSB ISH after the TLBI completes the invalidate; the
197	    // trailing ISB context-synchronizes before the subsequent eret.
198	    unsafe {
199	        asm!(
200	            "msr ttbr0_el1, {ttbr}",
201	            "isb",
202	            "tlbi aside1, {asid_x}",
203	            "dsb ish",
204	            "isb",
205	            ttbr = in(reg) tagged,
206	            asid_x = in(reg) (asid as u64) << 48,
207	            options(nomem, nostack, preserves_flags),
208	        );
209	    }
210	}
211	
212	/// Make instruction memory coherent with a recent data write at `va..va+len`.
213	///
214	/// AArch64 caches are PIPT for data but VIPT-flavored for instructions; after
215	/// writing into a code page we must clean the data side to PoU, then
216	/// invalidate the i-cache. Sequence is straight out of the ARM ARM §B2.4
217	/// example.
218	///
219	/// SAFETY: `va..va+len` must be a valid mapped range readable at EL1.
220	pub unsafe fn flush_icache_range(va: u64, len: usize) {
221	    // CTR_EL0.DminLine (bits 19:16) = log2 of the minimum D-cache line
222	    // size in words; multiply by the 4 B word size to get bytes. QEMU virt
223	    // reports 64 here; Apple Silicon reports 128.
224	    let ctr: u64;
225	    // SAFETY: CTR_EL0 is readable at EL1; no side effects.
226	    unsafe {
227	        asm!("mrs {0}, ctr_el0", out(reg) ctr, options(nomem, nostack, preserves_flags));
228	    }
229	    let line_bytes = 4_u64 << ((ctr >> 16) & 0xF);
230	
231	    let mut p = va & !(line_bytes - 1);
232	    let end = va + len as u64;
233	    while p < end {
234	        // SAFETY: `dc cvau` / `ic ivau` are EL0-permissive cache ops on a
235	        // VA already known to be mapped; no memory access semantically.
236	        unsafe {
237	            asm!("dc cvau, {0}", in(reg) p, options(nostack, preserves_flags));
238	        }
239	        p += line_bytes;
240	    }
241	    // SAFETY: barrier + i-cache invalidation; no memory access.
242	    unsafe {
243	        asm!(
244	            "dsb ish",
245	            "ic iallu",
246	            "dsb ish",
247	            "isb",
248	            options(nomem, nostack, preserves_flags),
249	        );
250	    }
251	}

+/// exception, passing `(ESR_EL1, ELR_EL1, FAR_EL1)`. It returns for faults
+/// it resolves; for anything it can't, it tail-calls
+/// Flow: classify the fault, stash it in the proc's
+/// proc on `RTS_PAGEFAULT`, then — for this slice only — resolve heap-window
+/// faults inline (the kernel stands in for the not-yet-existing VM server).
+/// `el1_return_to_user`, so the now-runnable faulting proc is rescheduled
+/// and retries the aborting instruction (aarch64 leaves `ELR_EL1` at the
+/// Slice 3.4 replaces the inline resolve with a kernel-originated
+/// `VM_PAGEFAULT` send: the proc then stays blocked on `RTS_PAGEFAULT`
+/// across the reschedule until VM answers with
+extern "C" fn do_page_fault(esr: u64, elr: u64, far: u64) {
+    // Only EL0 instruction (0x20) / data (0x24) aborts are faults we try to
+    // resolve; any other non-SVC sync exception is a genuine bug.
+    // Arch-neutral classification for `page_fault_state` (read by VM in 3.3+).
+    // SAFETY: exception context — single-threaded, DAIF.I masked. The
+    // faulting proc is CURRENT and not otherwise borrowed.
+    let p = unsafe { sched::current_proc_mut() }.expect("page fault: no current proc");
+    p.page_fault_state = PageFaultState { addr: far, flags, ip: elr };
+    // SAFETY: rts_set captures `nr` then ends the &mut Proc borrow before
+    // No VM yet (slice 3.4 sends VM_PAGEFAULT here); a fault outside the
+    // kernel-resolved heap window is unrecoverable → halt with the decoder.
+    // --- kernel-as-VM resolution (slice 3.4 lifts this into the VM server) ---
+    let frame = alloc_frame().expect("page fault: out of frames");
+    // SAFETY: ASID-tagged TLBI; the faulting proc's TTBR0 is the live one.
+        "[pf] proc={} far={:#x} -> alloc frame={:#x}, map RW, retry",
+    // SAFETY: rts_unset captures `nr` then ends the borrow before enqueue.
/// Tiny FSC-name decoder. Covers the codes a stub on a fresh AddrSpace
/// would actually hit. The full table (ARM ARM D13.2.40) has ~30 codes;
/// we just want boot-log triage to be tractable. Slice 3.2's real PF

toolUseResult.originalFile:
/*
 * MINIX 4 aarch64 EL0 → EL1 trap entry + eret stub.
 *
 * Used for:
 *   - EL0 SVC traps (vector slot 8). `vectors.S` branches here unconditionally
 *     for that slot; this stub saves user state into CURRENT_PROC->regs,
 *     decodes ESR_EL1, and dispatches either `do_ipc` (EC = SVC64) or the
 *     unexpected-sync panic helper.
 *   - Returning to EL0. `switch_to_user` and the SVC tail both branch to
 *     `el1_return_to_user`, which restores `tpidr_el1`-pointed regs and erets.
 *
 * Calling convention:
 *   - TPIDR_EL1 always holds `&mut CURRENT_PROC->regs` while we are running
 *     a user context. `switch_to_user` sets it before the first eret; we
 *     never clear it.
 *   - Field offsets below must match `kernel/src/arch/aarch64/context.rs`.
 *     The .equ block is mirrored from the Rust REGS_*_OFFSET constants; the
 *     `const _: () = assert!(size_of::<ArchRegisterFrame> == 272)` canary in
 *     context.rs catches drift in the trailing fields.
 */

    .equ REGS_X_OFFSET,      0
    .equ REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET, 248
    .equ REGS_ELR_OFFSET,    256
    .equ REGS_SPSR_OFFSET,   264

    .equ ESR_EC_SVC64,       0x15

    .section .text.trap, "ax", %progbits

/*
 * el0_64_sync_entry — entry stub for vector slot 8 (EL0 AArch64 sync).
 *
 * On entry:
 *   - EL1h, SP = SP_EL1 (kernel stack) is live and usable.
 *   - All EL0 user regs x0..x30 hold caller values, unsaved.
 *   - TPIDR_EL1 = &mut CURRENT_PROC->regs.
 *   - ESR/ELR/SPSR_EL1 describe the trap.
 *
 * We spill x29/x30 to the kernel stack to free two scratch registers
 * (x29 will be the frame pointer; x30 is reloaded from the stack into a
 * scratch reg before being written into the frame). All other user regs
 * are saved directly into the frame via stp/str, then SP_EL0 / ELR_EL1 /
 * SPSR_EL1 follow. EC is decoded from ESR_EL1; SVC64 → do_ipc, anything
 * else → el0_sync_unexpected.
 */
    .global el0_64_sync_entry
    .type el0_64_sync_entry, %function
el0_64_sync_entry:
    // Free x29/x30 for use as scratch (frame pointer + temporary).
    stp     x29, x30, [sp, #-16]!

    // x29 = &frame.
    mrs     x29, tpidr_el1

    // Save x0..x28 into the frame. x29 (frame ptr) cannot be its own base
    // and destination simultaneously, so we save the user's x29/x30 below.
    stp     x0,  x1,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
    stp     x2,  x3,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
    stp     x4,  x5,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
    stp     x6,  x7,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
    stp     x8,  x9,  [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
    stp     x10, x11, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
    stp     x12, x13, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
    stp     x14, x15, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
    stp     x18, x19, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
    stp     x20, x21, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
    stp     x22, x23, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
    stp     x24, x25, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
    stp     x26, x27, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
    str     x28,      [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]

    // Pop the user's x29 and x30 off the kernel stack into scratch regs
    // and store them into the frame.
    ldp     x16, x17, [sp], #16
    stp     x16, x17, [x29, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE8]

    // Save SP_EL0, ELR_EL1, SPSR_EL1.
    mrs     x9,  sp_el0
    mrs     x10, elr_el1
    mrs     x11, spsr_el1
    stp     x9,  x10, [x29, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]
    str     x11,      [x29, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]

    // Decode ESR_EL1 EC and dispatch.
    mrs     x12, esr_el1
    lsr     x13, x12, #26
    cmp     x13, #ESR_EC_SVC64
    b.ne    1f

    // SVC64 — call do_ipc(&mut frame), then run the slice-2.5 SVC tail
    // (schedule the next runnable proc + flush MF_DELIVERMSG into its
    // user buffer). The tail may transparently swap TPIDR_EL1 to a
    // different proc; the subsequent `el1_return_to_user` reads the new
    // TPIDR_EL1 and restores that frame.
    mov     x0, x29
    bl      do_ipc
    bl      el1_svc_tail
    b       el1_return_to_user

1:  // Non-SVC EL0 sync exception — diagnose and panic.
    // Args: el0_sync_unexpected(esr, elr, far).
    mov     x0, x12              // esr_el1
    mrs     x1, elr_el1
    mrs     x2, far_el1
    bl      el0_sync_unexpected
    // el0_sync_unexpected is -> !; if it ever returns, halt.
2:  wfe
    b       2b
    .size el0_64_sync_entry, . - el0_64_sync_entry

/*
 * el1_return_to_user — restore TPIDR_EL1-pointed frame and eret.
 *
 * Used by:
 *   - proc::sched::switch_to_user (first entry into EL0).
 *   - SVC tail (after do_ipc returns).
 *
 * Both paths leave TPIDR_EL1 pointing at the frame. x9 stays the base
 * throughout; x8 and x9 are restored last (otherwise loading into x9
 * would clobber the base).
 */
    .global el1_return_to_user
    .type el1_return_to_user, %function
el1_return_to_user:
    mrs     x9, tpidr_el1

    // System registers first.
    ldp     x10, x11, [x9, #REGS_SP_EL0_OFFSET]   // sp_el0, elr_el1
    ldr     x12,      [x9, #REGS_SPSR_OFFSET]
    msr     sp_el0,   x10
    msr     elr_el1,  x11
    msr     spsr_el1, x12

    // GPRs. Skip x8/x9 here — they'd clobber the base mid-restore.
    ldp     x0,  x1,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x00]
    ldp     x2,  x3,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x10]
    ldp     x4,  x5,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x20]
    ldp     x6,  x7,  [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x30]
    ldp     x10, x11, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x50]
    ldp     x12, x13, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x60]
    ldp     x14, x15, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x70]
    ldp     x16, x17, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x80]
    ldp     x18, x19, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x90]
    ldp     x20, x21, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xA0]
    ldp     x22, x23, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xB0]
    ldp     x24, x25, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xC0]
    ldp     x26, x27, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xD0]
    ldp     x28, x29, [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xE0]
    ldr     x30,      [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0xF0]

    // Finally x8 and x9 — load x9 last so the base survives until needed.
    ldr     x8,       [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x40]
    ldr     x9,       [x9, #REGS_X_OFFSET + 0x48]

    eret
    .size el1_return_to_user, . - el1_return_to_user

16	 *   endpoint with m_type = SYS_GETINFO and payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI in

18	 *   and name back into the payload; the reply's m_type is OK (0).

file.content:
/*
 * EL0 stubs for slice 2.5 ping-pong demo.
 *
 * Two PC-relative-free blobs, each copied into its own physical page by
 * `userland_bootstrap` (mapped EL0 RX at USER_CODE_VA_{A,B}). The stubs
 * exercise all three sync IPC primitives:
 *
 *   Stub A — sender: SENDREC to endpoint B in a loop, incrementing a
 *   counter held in x19 (AAPCS callee-saved; the SVC entry/exit code in
 *   trap.S saves and restores all of x0..x30, so x19 survives the trap).
 *
 *   Stub B — echo server: RECEIVE from ANY, then SEND back to whoever
 *   m_source identifies. Mirrors m_type unchanged.
 *
 *   Stub C — kernel-call client (slice 2.6): SENDREC to the SYSTEM
 *   endpoint with m_type = SYS_GETINFO and payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI in
 *   a loop. The kernel dispatcher writes endpoint, priv-flags, init-flags,
 *   and name back into the payload; the reply's m_type is OK (0).
 *
 * Each stub carves its 104-byte Message buffer out of [SP_EL0 - 104,
 * SP_EL0). SP stays 16-byte aligned (104 rounds to nothing modulo 16;
 * we never sub more than 104 so the stack top remains usable). The stack
 * pages are 4 KiB so we have headroom.
 *
 * Endpoint constants below are the boot endpoints of slots 11, 12, and
 * the SYSTEM kernel task (-2). make_endpoint(0, p) = p & 0x7FFF, so
 * SYSTEM at gen 0 = (-2 & 0x7FFF) = 0x7FFE. ANY's encoding is
 * `(1 << 14) - 1 = 0x3FFF` (ENDPOINT_SLOT_TOP at generation 0).
 */

    .equ SEND,         1
    .equ RECEIVE,      2
    .equ SENDREC,      3
    .equ ANY,          0x3FFF
    .equ ENDPOINT_A,   11
    .equ ENDPOINT_B,   12
    .equ ENDPOINT_SYS, 0x7FFE              // boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)
    .equ SYS_GETINFO,  0x600               // KERNEL_CALL + 0
    .equ GET_WHOAMI,   12
    .equ MSG_SIZE,     104                 // sizeof(Message); fits in `mov` imm12

/* ---- Stub A: sender ----------------------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_a, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_a_start
    .type _user_stub_a_start, %object
_user_stub_a_start:
    mov     x19, #0                         // persistent message counter
1:
    // Build Message at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0): m_source=0, m_type=counter.
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    str     wzr, [x9, #0]                   // m_source — kernel overwrites
    str     w19, [x9, #4]                   // m_type   — counter
    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_B
    mov     x1, #SENDREC
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    add     x19, x19, #1
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_a_end
_user_stub_a_end:
    .size _user_stub_a_start, _user_stub_a_end - _user_stub_a_start

/* ---- Stub B: echo server ----------------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_b, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_b_start
    .type _user_stub_b_start, %object
_user_stub_b_start:
1:
    // Build Message at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0). Kernel fills it on RECEIVE.
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    mov     x0, #ANY
    mov     x1, #RECEIVE
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    // Reply: read m_source out of the delivered message, SEND back with
    // the same m_type already in [x9 + 4].
    ldr     w10, [x9, #0]                   // m_source (signed Endpoint)
    sxtw    x10, w10
    mov     x0, x10
    mov     x1, #SEND
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_b_end
_user_stub_b_end:
    .size _user_stub_b_start, _user_stub_b_end - _user_stub_b_start

/* ---- Stub C: kernel-call client --------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_c, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_c_start
    .type _user_stub_c_start, %object
_user_stub_c_start:
    mov     x19, #0                         // persistent call counter
1:
    // Build the request at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0):
    //   [+0]   m_source — kernel overwrites
    //   [+4]   m_type    = SYS_GETINFO
    //   [+8]   payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    str     wzr, [x9, #0]
    mov     w10, #SYS_GETINFO
    str     w10, [x9, #4]
    mov     w10, #GET_WHOAMI
    str     w10, [x9, #8]
    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_SYS
    mov     x1, #SENDREC
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    add     x19, x19, #1
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_c_end
_user_stub_c_end:
    .size _user_stub_c_start, _user_stub_c_end - _user_stub_c_start

*   Stub C — kernel-call client (slice 2.6): SENDREC to the SYSTEM
 *   endpoint with m_type = SYS_GETINFO and payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI in
 *   a loop. The kernel dispatcher writes endpoint, priv-flags, init-flags,
 *   and name back into the payload; the reply's m_type is OK (0).
 *
 *   Stub D — page-fault demo (slice 3.2): stores to an unmapped page in

 *   kernel-as-VM handler allocates a frame, maps it RW, and unblocks the
 *   stub, which retries and then loops over the now-mapped page (no SVC,
 *   no further faults).

toolUseResult.originalFile:
/*
 * EL0 stubs for slice 2.5 ping-pong demo.
 *
 * Two PC-relative-free blobs, each copied into its own physical page by
 * `userland_bootstrap` (mapped EL0 RX at USER_CODE_VA_{A,B}). The stubs
 * exercise all three sync IPC primitives:
 *
 *   Stub A — sender: SENDREC to endpoint B in a loop, incrementing a
 *   counter held in x19 (AAPCS callee-saved; the SVC entry/exit code in
 *   trap.S saves and restores all of x0..x30, so x19 survives the trap).
 *
 *   Stub B — echo server: RECEIVE from ANY, then SEND back to whoever
 *   m_source identifies. Mirrors m_type unchanged.
 *
 *   Stub C — kernel-call client (slice 2.6): SENDREC to the SYSTEM
 *   endpoint with m_type = SYS_GETINFO and payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI in
 *   a loop. The kernel dispatcher writes endpoint, priv-flags, init-flags,
 *   and name back into the payload; the reply's m_type is OK (0).
 *
 * Each stub carves its 104-byte Message buffer out of [SP_EL0 - 104,
 * SP_EL0). SP stays 16-byte aligned (104 rounds to nothing modulo 16;
 * we never sub more than 104 so the stack top remains usable). The stack
 * pages are 4 KiB so we have headroom.
 *
 * Endpoint constants below are the boot endpoints of slots 11, 12, and
 * the SYSTEM kernel task (-2). make_endpoint(0, p) = p & 0x7FFF, so
 * SYSTEM at gen 0 = (-2 & 0x7FFF) = 0x7FFE. ANY's encoding is
 * `(1 << 14) - 1 = 0x3FFF` (ENDPOINT_SLOT_TOP at generation 0).
 */

    .equ SEND,         1
    .equ RECEIVE,      2
    .equ SENDREC,      3
    .equ ANY,          0x3FFF
    .equ ENDPOINT_A,   11
    .equ ENDPOINT_B,   12
    .equ ENDPOINT_SYS, 0x7FFE              // boot_endpoint(SYSTEM)
    .equ SYS_GETINFO,  0x600               // KERNEL_CALL + 0
    .equ GET_WHOAMI,   12
    .equ MSG_SIZE,     104                 // sizeof(Message); fits in `mov` imm12

/* ---- Stub A: sender ----------------------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_a, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_a_start
    .type _user_stub_a_start, %object
_user_stub_a_start:
    mov     x19, #0                         // persistent message counter
1:
    // Build Message at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0): m_source=0, m_type=counter.
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    str     wzr, [x9, #0]                   // m_source — kernel overwrites
    str     w19, [x9, #4]                   // m_type   — counter
    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_B
    mov     x1, #SENDREC
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    add     x19, x19, #1
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_a_end
_user_stub_a_end:
    .size _user_stub_a_start, _user_stub_a_end - _user_stub_a_start

/* ---- Stub B: echo server ----------------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_b, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_b_start
    .type _user_stub_b_start, %object
_user_stub_b_start:
1:
    // Build Message at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0). Kernel fills it on RECEIVE.
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    mov     x0, #ANY
    mov     x1, #RECEIVE
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    // Reply: read m_source out of the delivered message, SEND back with
    // the same m_type already in [x9 + 4].
    ldr     w10, [x9, #0]                   // m_source (signed Endpoint)
    sxtw    x10, w10
    mov     x0, x10
    mov     x1, #SEND
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_b_end
_user_stub_b_end:
    .size _user_stub_b_start, _user_stub_b_end - _user_stub_b_start

/* ---- Stub C: kernel-call client --------------------------------------- */

    .section .rodata.user_stub_c, "a", %progbits
    .balign 4
    .global _user_stub_c_start
    .type _user_stub_c_start, %object
_user_stub_c_start:
    mov     x19, #0                         // persistent call counter
1:
    // Build the request at [SP_EL0 - 104, SP_EL0):
    //   [+0]   m_source — kernel overwrites
    //   [+4]   m_type    = SYS_GETINFO
    //   [+8]   payload[0..4] = GET_WHOAMI
    sub     x9, sp, #MSG_SIZE
    str     wzr, [x9, #0]
    mov     w10, #SYS_GETINFO
    str     w10, [x9, #4]
    mov     w10, #GET_WHOAMI
    str     w10, [x9, #8]
    mov     x0, #ENDPOINT_SYS
    mov     x1, #SENDREC
    mov     x2, x9
    svc     #0
    add     x19, x19, #1
    b       1b
    .global _user_stub_c_end
_user_stub_c_end:
    .size _user_stub_c_start, _user_stub_c_end - _user_stub_c_start

input.new_string:
- **Slice 3.2** ◀ ready (branch `feature/phase-3-2-page-fault-handler`,
  pending merge) — Real EL0 page-fault handler + `RTS_PAGEFAULT` +
  kernel-resolved heap-window faults + 4th stub D. New
  `kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs` carries arch-neutral `PageFaultState`
  (`addr`/`flags`/`ip`; flag bits `PFF_WRITE`/`PFF_INSTR`/`PFF_PERMISSION`)
  and `HeapWindow { start, end }` with a `contains` helper; `Proc` gains a
  `page_fault_state` + `heap_window` block between `asid` and `next_ready`
  (`Proc::EMPTY` zeroes both). `arch/aarch64/exception.rs` adds
  `do_page_fault(esr, elr, far)`: it classifies the abort (EC 0x20/0x24,
  FSC, WnR), records `page_fault_state`, blocks the faulting proc on the
  3.1b `RTS_PAGEFAULT` bit via `sched::rts_set`, and — since no VM exists
  yet — resolves heap-window faults inline (kernel-as-VM): `alloc_frame`,
  new `addrspace::map_page_in(ttbr0_pa, …)` (the extracted `map_page` body,
  reused so the kernel can map into a live tree by root PA), new
  `mmu::flush_tlb_asid(asid)` (ASID-tagged TLBI without a TTBR0 write),
  then `sched::rts_unset` requeues the proc. Faults outside the window
  still halt via the verbatim 3.1b `el0_sync_unexpected` decoder. `trap.S`'s
  non-SVC sync arm now mirrors the SVC tail (`bl do_page_fault; bl
  el1_svc_tail; b el1_return_to_user`) so the unblocked proc is rescheduled
  and retries the aborting instruction (aarch64 leaves `ELR_EL1` on it).
  `user_stub.S` gains a `.rodata.user_stub_d` blob (store to `0x0100_0000`
  in a loop, no SVC); `userland.rs` wires stub D (ProcNr 14, PrivId 19,
  code `0x43_0000` / stack `0x83_0000`, heap window `[0x0100_0000,
  0x0100_4000)`, `trap_mask = TSK_T` — D does no IPC) and threads a
  `heap_window` arg through `build_stub` / `populate_stub_slot` (A/B/C pass
  `HeapWindow::EMPTY`). `kernel-shared` untouched; 26 host tests stay
  green. Verified in QEMU over 8 s: four `[as]` lines (D = ttbr0_pa
  `0x40015000` / asid 4), exactly one `[pf] proc=D far=0x1000000 → alloc
  frame=0x4001c000, map RW, retry`, then D round-robins; A↔B ping-pong
  (1732 `[ipc]`) and stub C SYS_GETINFO (1720 `[ksys]`) all `result=0`;
  zero panic / `el0_sync_unexpected` lines.

content.text:
Slice 3.2 is implemented, building clean, and verified in QEMU. All seven tasks done.

## Summary

**What changed** (branch `feature/phase-3-2-page-fault-handler`):
- **`proc/page_fault.rs`** (new) — arch-neutral `PageFaultState` + `HeapWindow` + `PFF_*` flags.
- **`proc/proc_struct.rs`** — `Proc` gains `page_fault_state` + `heap_window` (zeroed in `EMPTY`).
- **`mmu.rs`** — `flush_tlb_asid(asid)` (ASID-tagged TLBI, no TTBR0 write).
- **`addrspace.rs`** — extracted `map_page_in(ttbr0_pa, …)` free fn; `map_page` delegates.
- **`exception.rs`** — `do_page_fault(esr, elr, far)`: classify → record → `rts_set(RTS_PAGEFAULT)` → resolve heap-window faults inline (alloc + map + TLBI) → `rts_unset`; out-of-window faults fall through to the verbatim 3.1b halt decoder.
- **`trap.S`** — non-SVC sync arm now mirrors the SVC tail (`do_page_fault; el1_svc_tail; el1_return_to_user`).
- **`user_stub.S` / `userland.rs`** — stub D (ProcNr 14, heap window `[0x0100_0000, 0x0100_4000)`, no IPC); `heap_window` threaded through `build_stub`/`populate_stub_slot`.
- **`docs/plan.md`** — 3.1b → shipped (PR #10), 3.2 → ready, 3.3 → next.

**Verification** (QEMU, 8s → `/tmp/phase3.log`):
- 4 `[as]` lines incl. `stub D nr=14 ttbr0_pa=0x40015000 asid=4`.
- Exactly one `[pf] proc=D far=0x1000000 → alloc frame=0x4001c000, map RW, retry`, then D loops without re-faulting.
- Regressions intact: 1732 `[ipc]` (A↔B) + 1720 `[ksys]` (C), all `result=0`; zero panics/aborts.
- 26 `kernel-shared` host tests green.

**Note on a design choice worth your eyes:** the decomposition plan's "no allocation in fault context" is forward-looking for the 3.4 VM handoff. For 3.2 I resolve inline inside `do_page_fault` (single core, IRQs masked → equivalent), but keep the block/record half (`RTS_PAGEFAULT` + `page_fault_state`) real and separable so 3.4 can swap the inline resolve for a `VM_PAGEFAULT` send.

Changes are staged in the working tree, **not committed** (auto mode). Before a PR, the pre-PR checklist calls for a CLAUDE.md review pass — want me to run `/claude-md-management:revise-claude-md`, and then commit + open the PR?

Review this session for learnings about working with Claude Code in this codebase. Update CLAUDE.md with context that would help future Claude sessions be more effective.

## Step 1: Reflect

What context was missing that would have helped Claude work more effectively?
- Bash commands that were used or discovered
- Code style patterns followed
- Testing approaches that worked
- Environment/configuration quirks
- Warnings or gotchas encountered

## Step 2: Find CLAUDE.md Files

```bash
find . -name "CLAUDE.md" -o -name ".claude.local.md" 2>/dev/null | head -20
```

Decide where each addition belongs:
- `CLAUDE.md` - Team-shared (checked into git)
- `.claude.local.md` - Personal/local only (gitignored)

## Step 3: Draft Additions

**Keep it concise** - one line per concept. CLAUDE.md is part of the prompt, so brevity matters.

Format: `<command or pattern>` - `<brief description>`

Avoid:
- Verbose explanations
- Obvious information
- One-off fixes unlikely to recur

## Step 4: Show Proposed Changes

For each addition:

```
### Update: ./CLAUDE.md

**Why:** [one-line reason]

\`\`\`diff
+ [the addition - keep it brief]
\`\`\`
```

## Step 5: Apply with Approval

Ask if the user wants to apply the changes. Only edit files they approve.

Let me reflect on this session, then find the CLAUDE.md files.

This slice followed the existing CLAUDE.md conventions closely (NLL borrow discipline, `rts_set`/`rts_unset`, forward-decl `#![allow(dead_code)]`, `.S` files, QEMU-with-timeout verification) — those all worked without friction. content.text:
Single CLAUDE.md at the repo root. Here are my two proposed additions — both non-obvious, recurring, and not currently captured.

---

### Update 1: `./CLAUDE.md` (Code Conventions)

**Why:** The kernel only ever builds `--release`, so `debug_assert!` is silently compiled out. I hit this reasoning in `mmu.rs` (hard `assert!` guards "because debug_assert! is compiled out"). Future sessions writing invariant checks need to know which to reach for.

```diff
+ - The kernel ships `--release` only, so `debug_assert!` is compiled out. Use a hard `assert!` for invariants whose violation would silently corrupt (e.g. a null TTBR0/ASID reaching the scheduler); reserve `debug_assert!` for cheap "can't happen" documentation that's fine to drop in release
```

### Update 2: `./CLAUDE.md` (Build section)

**Why:** The QEMU boot log interleaves raw single-char clock-tick bytes (`A`/`B`/`C`/`D`) with structured lines, so plain `grep` reports "Binary file matches" and hides results. I used `grep -a` throughout this session's verification.

```diff
  # Boot in QEMU (cargo runner wires tools/qemu-run.sh). The kernel runs
  # indefinitely once EL0 starts (slice 2.4+), so `timeout` is mandatory.
  # Redirect to a file when you need to grep tick output -- live tail loses lines.
+ # The log interleaves raw single-char tick bytes; grep it with `grep -a`
+ # (force text) or matches read as "Binary file matches".
  timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release
```

---

Everything else this slice followed conventions already documented (borrow discipline, forward-decl `#![allow(dead_code)]`, `.S` build wiring, doc-marker updates). Want me to apply both, one, or neither — then I'll commit and open the PR?

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# MINIX 4

MINIX 4 is a learning OS with a Rust microkernel, based on MINIX 3's architecture.

## Project Overview

- **Kernel:** Rust (no_std, no_main), greenfield microkernel
- **Architecture:** aarch64 primary (Apple Silicon / QEMU virt), x86_64 secondary
- **Servers:** PM, VFS, VM, RS, DS, SCHED as user-space Rust processes
- **Drivers:** VirtIO (MMIO for aarch64, PCI for x86_64) as user-space processes
- **C library:** musl-libc fork with MINIX IPC syscall wrappers
- **License:** BSD-2-Clause only (no GPL code)
- **Platform:** QEMU as primary target

## Reference Codebase

MINIX 4's architecture is based on MINIX 3. When the docs reference "MINIX 3 source",
they mean paths within the MINIX 3 source tree (e.g., `kernel/proc.c` means the
`kernel/proc.c` file in a MINIX 3 checkout). Key reference files:

- `kernel/proc.c` -- IPC implementation (mini_send, mini_receive, deadlock detection)
- `kernel/proc.h`, `priv.h` -- Process and privilege structures
- `kernel/system.c` -- Kernel call dispatch
- `include/minix/ipc.h` -- Message structure definitions
- `include/minix/com.h`, `callnr.h` -- Server endpoints, call numbers
- `lib/libc/sys/*.c` -- POSIX syscall wrappers (template for musl adaptation)
- `lib/libsys/sef.c` -- SEF framework (template for server-rt)

The MINIX 3 source is available at https://github.com/Stichting-MINIX-Research-Foundation/minix

## Build

```sh
# Build kernel for aarch64 (primary target)
cargo kernel-aarch64

# Boot in QEMU (cargo runner wires tools/qemu-run.sh). The kernel runs
# indefinitely once EL0 starts (slice 2.4+), so `timeout` is mandatory.
# Redirect to a file when you need to grep tick output -- live tail loses lines.
timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release

# Build kernel for x86_64
cargo kernel-x86_64
```

## Architecture

See `docs/architecture.md` for the full system design. Key concepts:

- **Microkernel:** Only IPC, scheduling, interrupt dispatch, and memory protection in kernel
- **Message passing:** 6 IPC primitives (SEND, RECEIVE, SENDREC, NOTIFY, SENDNB, SENDA)
- **User-space servers:** All OS services (file system, process management, memory management) run as separate processes communicating via IPC
- **Privilege model:** Fine-grained bitmaps control which processes can communicate and what kernel calls they can make

## Workspace Crates

| Crate | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `kernel` | Microkernel (no_std) |
| `kernel-shared` | Message types, endpoints, call numbers shared between kernel and userspace |
| `minix-ipc` | User-space IPC library (SVC/SYSCALL asm stubs) |
| `server-rt` | Server runtime / SEF framework |
| `servers/*` | System servers (PM, VFS, VM, RS, DS, SCHED) |
| `drivers/*` | Device drivers (VirtIO block/net/console, memory) |
| `fs/*` | File system servers (MFS, PFS) |
| `userland/*` | User programs (init, sh, coreutils) |

## Code Conventions

- Kernel `unsafe` blocks require `// SAFETY:` comments documenting the invariant
- IPC linked lists use `Option<ProcNr>` indices into static arrays, not raw pointers
- Message types are defined in `kernel-shared` and shared across all crates
- Assembly is confined to `.S` files (assembled via `cc` crate in `build.rs`); use `core::arch::asm!` only for single-instruction operations
- New `.S` files must be added to `kernel/build.rs`'s `sources` array; offset blocks (`.equ REGS_*_OFFSET …`) are duplicated per-file since there is no cross-`.S` include
- To end a `&mut` borrow before an `unsafe` call that re-borrows the same static, capture state into locals (bool / scalar) and rely on NLL — `drop(&mut x)` is a no-op and triggers a `dropping_references` warning
- Run-queue admission is decoupled from boot: `IMAGE.runnable` marks IPC reachability; only `proc::sched::enqueue` puts a proc in the scheduler's run queue
- Static mutable tables use `UnsafeCell<[T; N]>` inside a `#[repr(transparent)]` newtype with `unsafe impl Sync`; document the single-threaded-boot invariant in the `// SAFETY:` comment
- Custom `Display` impls that must honor `{:<width$}` render through a stack buffer (`arrayvec::ArrayString<N>`) and call `f.pad(s)` — `write!(f, ...)` from inside `Display::fmt` ignores the outer width spec
- Forward declarations intended for later slices (constants, fields, re-exports) get module-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` with a one-line comment naming the consuming slice
- IPC primitives take an explicit `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]` (and `&mut [Priv; NR_SYS_PROCS]`) slice; only `ipc::do_ipc` materializes those from `PROC_TABLE` / `PRIV_TABLE` via `proc_table_mut_slice` / `priv_table_mut_slice`. Keeps each primitive testable in isolation and dodges the two-`&mut`-from-one-`UnsafeCell` UB hazard
- Every EL1 → EL0 transition (SVC tail via `el1_svc_tail`, `sched::reschedule`, `sched::run`) calls `sched::schedule_next`, which flushes `Proc::deliver_msg` to the user buffer at `Proc::deliver_msg_vir` and clears `MF_DELIVERMSG` before resuming
- IPC blocking pairs with the new `sched::rts_set` / `rts_unset` helpers — they capture `nr`, end the `&mut Proc` borrow, then call `enqueue` / `dequeue` so RTS state and the run queue stay in sync. Same NLL-capture pattern slice 2.4 used in `clock::tick`
- `kernel/build.rs` skips assembly when `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS != "none"` so `cargo check --workspace` / `cargo test --workspace` keep working on host. The kernel's real modules are gated by `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` in `main.rs` regardless
- `cargo test -p minix4-kernel` runs zero tests by design — every kernel module is gated on `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` and host-test infra is not yet built; host-runnable tests live in `kernel-shared`. QEMU is the primary verification for kernel code (`timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release`)

## Documentation

`docs/plan.md` tracks slice status with three markers: `◀ next` (unstarted), `◀ ready (branch ..., pending merge)` (implemented but unmerged), `✓ shipped (PR #N, merged YYYY-MM-DD)` (merged). Flip the previous slice forward and slide `◀ next` ahead as part of each slice's PR. When opening a new slice PR, also reconcile any older `◀ ready` markers against `git log` — stale "pending merge" labels on already-merged PRs accumulate otherwise.

+# The log interleaves raw single-char tick bytes; grep it with `grep -a`
+# (force text) or matches read as "Binary file matches".

80	- Custom `Display` impls that must honor `{:<width$}` render through a stack buffer (`arrayvec::ArrayString<N>`) and call `f.pad(s)` — `write!(f, ...)` from inside `Display::fmt` ignores the outer width spec
81	- Forward declarations intended for later slices (constants, fields, re-exports) get module-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` with a one-line comment naming the consuming slice
82	- IPC primitives take an explicit `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]` (and `&mut [Priv; NR_SYS_PROCS]`) slice; only `ipc::do_ipc` materializes those from `PROC_TABLE` / `PRIV_TABLE` via `proc_table_mut_slice` / `priv_table_mut_slice`. Keeps each primitive testable in isolation and dodges the two-`&mut`-from-one-`UnsafeCell` UB hazard
83	- Every EL1 → EL0 transition (SVC tail via `el1_svc_tail`, `sched::reschedule`, `sched::run`) calls `sched::schedule_next`, which flushes `Proc::deliver_msg` to the user buffer at `Proc::deliver_msg_vir` and clears `MF_DELIVERMSG` before resuming
84	- IPC blocking pairs with the new `sched::rts_set` / `rts_unset` helpers — they capture `nr`, end the `&mut Proc` borrow, then call `enqueue` / `dequeue` so RTS state and the run queue stay in sync. Same NLL-capture pattern slice 2.4 used in `clock::tick`
85	- `kernel/build.rs` skips assembly when `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS != "none"` so `cargo check --workspace` / `cargo test --workspace` keep working on host. The kernel's real modules are gated by `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` in `main.rs` regardless
86	- `cargo test -p minix4-kernel` runs zero tests by design — every kernel module is gated on `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` and host-test infra is not yet built; host-runnable tests live in `kernel-shared`. QEMU is the primary verification for kernel code (`timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release`)
87	
88	## Documentation
89

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# MINIX 4

MINIX 4 is a learning OS with a Rust microkernel, based on MINIX 3's architecture.

## Project Overview

- **Kernel:** Rust (no_std, no_main), greenfield microkernel
- **Architecture:** aarch64 primary (Apple Silicon / QEMU virt), x86_64 secondary
- **Servers:** PM, VFS, VM, RS, DS, SCHED as user-space Rust processes
- **Drivers:** VirtIO (MMIO for aarch64, PCI for x86_64) as user-space processes
- **C library:** musl-libc fork with MINIX IPC syscall wrappers
- **License:** BSD-2-Clause only (no GPL code)
- **Platform:** QEMU as primary target

## Reference Codebase

MINIX 4's architecture is based on MINIX 3. When the docs reference "MINIX 3 source",
they mean paths within the MINIX 3 source tree (e.g., `kernel/proc.c` means the
`kernel/proc.c` file in a MINIX 3 checkout). Key reference files:

- `kernel/proc.c` -- IPC implementation (mini_send, mini_receive, deadlock detection)
- `kernel/proc.h`, `priv.h` -- Process and privilege structures
- `kernel/system.c` -- Kernel call dispatch
- `include/minix/ipc.h` -- Message structure definitions
- `include/minix/com.h`, `callnr.h` -- Server endpoints, call numbers
- `lib/libc/sys/*.c` -- POSIX syscall wrappers (template for musl adaptation)
- `lib/libsys/sef.c` -- SEF framework (template for server-rt)

The MINIX 3 source is available at https://github.com/Stichting-MINIX-Research-Foundation/minix

## Build

```sh
# Build kernel for aarch64 (primary target)
cargo kernel-aarch64

# Boot in QEMU (cargo runner wires tools/qemu-run.sh). The kernel runs
# indefinitely once EL0 starts (slice 2.4+), so `timeout` is mandatory.
# Redirect to a file when you need to grep tick output -- live tail loses lines.
# The log interleaves raw single-char tick bytes; grep it with `grep -a`
# (force text) or matches read as "Binary file matches".
timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release

# Build kernel for x86_64
cargo kernel-x86_64
```

## Architecture

See `docs/architecture.md` for the full system design. Key concepts:

- **Microkernel:** Only IPC, scheduling, interrupt dispatch, and memory protection in kernel
- **Message passing:** 6 IPC primitives (SEND, RECEIVE, SENDREC, NOTIFY, SENDNB, SENDA)
- **User-space servers:** All OS services (file system, process management, memory management) run as separate processes communicating via IPC
- **Privilege model:** Fine-grained bitmaps control which processes can communicate and what kernel calls they can make

## Workspace Crates

| Crate | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `kernel` | Microkernel (no_std) |
| `kernel-shared` | Message types, endpoints, call numbers shared between kernel and userspace |
| `minix-ipc` | User-space IPC library (SVC/SYSCALL asm stubs) |
| `server-rt` | Server runtime / SEF framework |
| `servers/*` | System servers (PM, VFS, VM, RS, DS, SCHED) |
| `drivers/*` | Device drivers (VirtIO block/net/console, memory) |
| `fs/*` | File system servers (MFS, PFS) |
| `userland/*` | User programs (init, sh, coreutils) |

## Code Conventions

- Kernel `unsafe` blocks require `// SAFETY:` comments documenting the invariant
- IPC linked lists use `Option<ProcNr>` indices into static arrays, not raw pointers
- Message types are defined in `kernel-shared` and shared across all crates
- Assembly is confined to `.S` files (assembled via `cc` crate in `build.rs`); use `core::arch::asm!` only for single-instruction operations
- New `.S` files must be added to `kernel/build.rs`'s `sources` array; offset blocks (`.equ REGS_*_OFFSET …`) are duplicated per-file since there is no cross-`.S` include
- To end a `&mut` borrow before an `unsafe` call that re-borrows the same static, capture state into locals (bool / scalar) and rely on NLL — `drop(&mut x)` is a no-op and triggers a `dropping_references` warning
- Run-queue admission is decoupled from boot: `IMAGE.runnable` marks IPC reachability; only `proc::sched::enqueue` puts a proc in the scheduler's run queue
- Static mutable tables use `UnsafeCell<[T; N]>` inside a `#[repr(transparent)]` newtype with `unsafe impl Sync`; document the single-threaded-boot invariant in the `// SAFETY:` comment
- Custom `Display` impls that must honor `{:<width$}` render through a stack buffer (`arrayvec::ArrayString<N>`) and call `f.pad(s)` — `write!(f, ...)` from inside `Display::fmt` ignores the outer width spec
- Forward declarations intended for later slices (constants, fields, re-exports) get module-level `#![allow(dead_code)]` with a one-line comment naming the consuming slice
- IPC primitives take an explicit `&mut [Proc; N_PROC_SLOTS]` (and `&mut [Priv; NR_SYS_PROCS]`) slice; only `ipc::do_ipc` materializes those from `PROC_TABLE` / `PRIV_TABLE` via `proc_table_mut_slice` / `priv_table_mut_slice`. Keeps each primitive testable in isolation and dodges the two-`&mut`-from-one-`UnsafeCell` UB hazard
- Every EL1 → EL0 transition (SVC tail via `el1_svc_tail`, `sched::reschedule`, `sched::run`) calls `sched::schedule_next`, which flushes `Proc::deliver_msg` to the user buffer at `Proc::deliver_msg_vir` and clears `MF_DELIVERMSG` before resuming
- IPC blocking pairs with the new `sched::rts_set` / `rts_unset` helpers — they capture `nr`, end the `&mut Proc` borrow, then call `enqueue` / `dequeue` so RTS state and the run queue stay in sync. Same NLL-capture pattern slice 2.4 used in `clock::tick`
- `kernel/build.rs` skips assembly when `CARGO_CFG_TARGET_OS != "none"` so `cargo check --workspace` / `cargo test --workspace` keep working on host. The kernel's real modules are gated by `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` in `main.rs` regardless
- `cargo test -p minix4-kernel` runs zero tests by design — every kernel module is gated on `#[cfg(target_os = "none")]` and host-test infra is not yet built; host-runnable tests live in `kernel-shared`. QEMU is the primary verification for kernel code (`timeout 8 cargo run -p minix4-kernel --target aarch64-unknown-none --release`)

## Documentation

`docs/plan.md` tracks slice status with three markers: `◀ next` (unstarted), `◀ ready (branch ..., pending merge)` (implemented but unmerged), `✓ shipped (PR #N, merged YYYY-MM-DD)` (merged). Flip the previous slice forward and slide `◀ next` ahead as part of each slice's PR. When opening a new slice PR, also reconcile any older `◀ ready` markers against `git log` — stale "pending merge" labels on already-merged PRs accumulate otherwise.

- Kernel `unsafe` blocks require `// SAFETY:` comments documenting the invariant
- IPC linked lists use `Option<ProcNr>` indices into static arrays, not raw pointers
- Message types are defined in `kernel-shared` and shared across all crates

input.command:
git add CLAUDE.md docs/plan.md kernel/src/arch/aarch64/addrspace.rs kernel/src/arch/aarch64/exception.rs kernel/src/arch/aarch64/mmu.rs kernel/src/arch/aarch64/trap.S kernel/src/arch/aarch64/user_stub.S kernel/src/arch/aarch64/userland.rs kernel/src/proc/mod.rs kernel/src/proc/proc_struct.rs kernel/src/proc/page_fault.rs && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
phase 3.2: real page-fault handler + RTS_PAGEFAULT + stub D

EL0 instruction/data aborts now route through do_page_fault instead of
panicking. The handler classifies the abort, records it in the new
Proc::page_fault_state, and blocks the proc on RTS_PAGEFAULT via
sched::rts_set. With no VM server yet, the kernel resolves heap-window
faults inline: alloc_frame, map RW via the new addrspace::map_page_in
(extracted map_page body, so a live tree can be mapped by root PA),
ASID-tagged TLBI via the new mmu::flush_tlb_asid, then rts_unset requeues
the proc. trap.S's non-SVC sync arm now mirrors the SVC tail
(do_page_fault; el1_svc_tail; el1_return_to_user) so the unblocked proc is
rescheduled and retries the aborting instruction. Faults outside the window
still halt via the verbatim 3.1b decoder.

New EL0 stub D (ProcNr 14) stores to an unmapped heap page in a loop to
exercise the path; build_stub/populate_stub_slot gain a heap_window arg
(A/B/C pass empty). page_fault.rs holds arch-neutral PageFaultState +
HeapWindow.

Verified in QEMU over 8s: four [as] lines (D asid=4), exactly one [pf]
line for D then it loops, A/B ping-pong (1732 [ipc]) and C SYS_GETINFO
(1720 [ksys]) all result=0, no panics. 26 kernel-shared host tests green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" && echo "--- post-commit status ---" && git status --short
